metadata.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

one_liner.achieved: 2-4× throughput improvement over state-of-the-art LLM serving systems
one_liner.via: PagedAttention algorithm that applies OS-style virtual memory paging to KV cache management
one_liner.because: dynamic block-level memory allocation eliminates fragmentation and enables sharing across requests, allowing more concurrent requests in memory

claim_tree.statement: Existing systems struggle because the key-value (KV) cache memory for each request is huge and grows and shrinks dynamically.

children.statement: The authors propose PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems.

claim.statement: The authors propose PagedAttention, an attention algorithm inspired by virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems.

universal_facets.analogy_source: virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems

algorithm_facets.g1: Attention algorithm for memory management in large language model serving.
algorithm_facets.g2: Key-value cache memory management and attention computation.
algorithm_facets.g3: Near-zero waste in KV cache memory and improved throughput.

children.statement: vLLM achieves near-zero waste in KV cache memory.
vLLM improves the throughput of popular LLMs by 2-4× compared to FasterTransformer and Orca at the same level of latency.
The throughput improvement of vLLM is more pronounced with longer sequences.
The ShareGPT dataset has 8.4 × longer input prompts and 5.8 × longer outputs on average than the Alpaca dataset, with higher variance.
vLLM can sustain 2 × higher request rate.
vLLM improves throughput over Orca baselines on OPT-13B and Alpaca dataset from 1.3x in basic sampling to 2.3x in beam search with a width of 6.
vLLM achieves 6.1% - 9.8% memory saving on parallel sampling and 37.6% - 55.2% on beam search on the Alpaca/OPT-13B experiments.
On the ShareGPT dataset, vLLM achieves 16.2% - 30.5% memory saving on parallel sampling and 44.3% - 66.3% on beam search.
vLLM achieves 1.67× higher throughput than Orca (Oracle) when the one-shot prefix is shared (1 example with 80 tokens).
vLLM achieves 3.58× higher throughput than Orca (Oracle) when 5 examples are shared (5 examples with 341 tokens).
vLLM can sustain 2× higher request rate on chatbot workload.
vLLM sustains 2 × higher request rates compared to the three Orca baselines on the ShareGPT chatbot workload using OPT-13B.
PagedAttention GPU kernels incur 20–26% higher attention kernel latency compared to FasterTransformer.
children.rejected_what: Lower attention kernel latency (relative to FasterTransformer)
children.rejected_why: Extra overheads of accessing the block table, executing extra branches, and handling variable sequence lengths

children.statement: Recomputation overhead is never higher than 20% of swapping's latency.
For medium block sizes from 16 to 64, the two methods exhibit comparable end-to-end performance.
Swapping incurs excessive overhead with small block sizes due to numerous small data transfers between CPU and GPU limiting PCIe bandwidth.
Recomputation overhead remains constant across different block sizes.
Recomputation is more efficient when the block size is small, while swapping is more efficient when the block size is large.

The idea of virtual memory and paging is effective for managing the KV cache in LLM serving because the workload requires dynamic memory allocation (since the output length is not known a priori) and its performance is bound by the GPU memory capacity.
children.rejected_what: Generalization to workloads with static memory allocation
children.rejected_why: Memory allocation can be optimized ahead of time

children.statement: However, this does not generally hold for every GPU workload. In such scenarios, introducing the vLLM’s techniques may rather degrade the performance due to the extra overhead of memory indirection and non-contiguous block memory.
children.rejected_what: Generalization to all GPU workloads (e.g., DNN training, non-LLM serving)
children.rejected_why: Static tensor shapes, compute-bound performance, overhead of memory indirection

children.statement: For example, in DNN training, the tensor shapes are typically static, and thus memory allocation can be optimized ahead of time.
children.rejected_what: Applicability of vLLM techniques to DNN training

children.statement: For another example, in serving DNNs that are not LLMs, an increase in memory efficiency may not result in any performance improvement since the performance is primarily compute-bound.
children.rejected_what: Performance improvement from memory efficiency in non-LLM serving

children.statement: In such scenarios, introducing the vLLM’s techniques may rather degrade the performance due to the extra overhead of memory indirection and non-contiguous block memory.
children.rejected_why: Extra overhead of memory indirection and non-contiguous block memory

children.statement: However, we would be excited to see vLLM’s techniques being applied to other workloads with similar properties to LLM serving.
children.rejected_what: Applicability to workloads without similar properties
children.rejected_why: Lack of similar properties to LLM serving

children.statement: Another example is the recomputation method to recover the evicted blocks, which is not feasible in OS.
Besides, vLLM mitigates the overhead of memory indirection in paging by fusing the GPU kernels for memory access operations with those for other operations such as attention.
children.rejected_what: Overhead of memory indirection in paging
children.rejected_why: Fusing GPU kernels for memory access operations

children.statement: One example is vLLM’s all-or-nothing swap-out policy, which exploits the fact that processing a request requires all of its corresponding token states to be stored in GPU memory.
children.rejected_why: Processing a request requires all of its corresponding token states to be stored in GPU memory

children.statement: vLLM re-interprets and augments the idea of virtual memory and paging by leveraging the applicationspecific semantics.
children.rejected_what: Standard virtual memory and paging idea
children.rejected_why: vLLM re-interprets and augments it by leveraging applicationspecific semantics

children.statement: Established techniques such as virtual memory and copy-on-write can be adapted to efficiently manage KV cache and handle various decoding algorithms in LLM serving.
PagedAttention allows attention keys and values to be stored in non-contiguous paged memory.
vLLM achieves 2-4 × throughput improvements over the state-of-the-art systems.
vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving system with efficient memory management enabled by PagedAttention.
The authors build vLLM, an LLM serving system based on PagedAttention.

claim.statement: The authors build vLLM, an LLM serving system based on PagedAttention.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Minimizes KV cache waste to maximize throughput
universal_facets.analogy_source: virtual memory and paging techniques in operating systems

systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV cache pages mapped to physical memory via paging techniques
systems_facets.s5_policy: Paging-inspired policy for dynamic KV cache management

children.statement: vLLM implements a copy-on-write mechanism at the block granularity to manage physical blocks shared across multiple output samples in parallel sampling.

claim.statement: vLLM implements a copy-on-write mechanism at the block granularity to manage physical blocks shared across multiple output samples in parallel sampling.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory sharing reduces usage, copy-on-write handles divergence.

systems_facets.s1_resource: Memory used for KV cache storage and management.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Physical blocks at block granularity for KV cache.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: LLM serving system layer managing memory and decoding.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical blocks mapped to physical blocks via PagedAttention.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Copy-on-write policy based on physical block reference counts.

children.statement: vLLM manages KV blocks for beam search by sharing blocks across candidates and freeing physical blocks when reference counts reach zero.
vLLM reserves physical blocks for predefined shared prefixes and maps logical blocks to cached physical blocks to reduce redundant computation.
vLLM facilitates simultaneous processing of requests with different decoding preferences via a common mapping layer that translates logical blocks to physical blocks.
vLLM adopts the first-come-first-serve (FCFS) scheduling policy for all requests to ensure fairness and prevent starvation.

claim.statement: vLLM adopts the first-come-first-serve (FCFS) scheduling policy for all requests to ensure fairness and prevent starvation.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: Improves handling of request traffic surpassing system capacity.
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Balances CPU swap bandwidth and GPU recomputation power against latency.
universal_facets.analogy_source: Virtual memory implementations for swapping evicted pages.

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory blocks and CPU RAM for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: System-level inference scheduler for LLM services.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence blocks mapped via copy to GPU or CPU physical blocks.
systems_facets.s5_policy: First-come-first-serve scheduling and all-or-nothing eviction policy.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU memory with sufficient bandwidth for swapping.

children.statement: vLLM preempts requests by serving the earliest arrived requests first and preempting the latest requests first.

claim.statement: vLLM preempts requests by serving the earliest arrived requests first and preempting the latest requests first.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: CPU RAM bandwidth vs GPU computation power

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory for KV cache and CPU RAM for swapping.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Requests and sequences within sequence groups.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Evicted sequence blocks mapped to CPU memory via swapping.
systems_facets.s5_policy: FCFS scheduling and all-or-nothing eviction policy.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU memory with bandwidth for swapping.

children.statement: vLLM implements an all-or-nothing eviction policy where all or none of the blocks of a sequence are evicted together because all blocks of a sequence are accessed together.

claim.statement: vLLM implements an all-or-nothing eviction policy where all or none of the blocks of a sequence are evicted together because all blocks of a sequence are accessed together.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory bandwidth vs. computation power for recovery

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks and CPU RAM for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Blocks of a sequence or sequence group for scheduling.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: LLM serving system layer managing inference requests and memory.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Sequence blocks map to GPU physical blocks or CPU RAM.
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction policy and first-come-first-serve scheduling policy.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU RAM with bandwidth between them.

children.statement: Sequences within one request (e.g., beam candidates) are gang-scheduled as a sequence group and are always preempted or rescheduled together.

claim.statement: Sequences within one request (e.g., beam candidates) are gang-scheduled as a sequence group and are always preempted or rescheduled together.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Memory sharing efficiency versus independent scheduling flexibility

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory blocks for KV cache and CPU RAM for swapped blocks.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Sequence group consisting of multiple sequences within one request.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: Scheduler layer within vLLM system managing request prioritization.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical sequence group maps to physical GPU blocks or CPU RAM.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Gang-scheduling policy where sequence groups are preempted or rescheduled together.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU and CPU memory architecture with bandwidth between them.

children.statement: vLLM uses a swapping technique that copies evicted blocks to CPU memory managed by a CPU block allocator when GPU physical blocks are exhausted.

claim.statement: vLLM uses a swapping technique that copies evicted blocks to CPU memory managed by a CPU block allocator when GPU physical blocks are exhausted.

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU physical blocks and CPU memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: KV cache blocks or sequences within a sequence group.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: LLM inference engine memory management layer.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV cache blocks mapped to physical CPU memory via swapping.
systems_facets.s5_policy: All-or-nothing eviction policy for sequences, FCFS for requests.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: Requires GPU and CPU RAM with sufficient memory bandwidth.

children.statement: vLLM supports the widely used Megatron-LM style tensor model parallelism strategy on Transformers for distributed execution.
vLLM features a single KV cache manager within the centralized scheduler that is shared by different GPU workers.

claim.statement: vLLM features a single KV cache manager within the centralized scheduler that is shared by different GPU workers.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Centralized memory management avoids worker synchronization overhead during execution.

systems_facets.s1_resource: KV cache memory managed by centralized scheduler.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Logical blocks mapped to physical blocks per request.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: System layer scheduler coordinating distributed GPU workers.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical blocks mapped to physical blocks via scheduler broadcast.
systems_facets.s5_policy: SPMD execution schedule with shared KV cache manager.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: Distributed GPUs supporting allreduce communication primitives.

children.statement: GPU workers synchronize intermediate results with the all-reduce communication primitive without the coordination of the scheduler.

claim.statement: GPU workers synchronize intermediate results with the all-reduce communication primitive without the coordination of the scheduler.

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU memory and KV cache for distributed model parallelism.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: GPU workers processing input tokens in batches via SPMD.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: System layer for distributed LLM inference execution.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical blocks mapped to physical blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: SPMD execution schedule governs distributed model parallelism.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: Multiple distributed GPUs such as A100 for model parallelism.

children.statement: GPU workers do not synchronize on memory management as they only need to receive all the memory management information at the beginning of each decoding iteration.

The authors fuse the reshape and block write operations into a single GPU kernel to minimize kernel launch overheads while splitting the new KV cache into blocks and saving them at positions specified by the block table.

The authors adapt the attention kernel from FasterTransformer to read KV cache according to the block table and perform attention operations on the fly, assigning a GPU warp to read each block to ensure coalesced memory access and supporting variable sequence lengths within a request batch.
The authors implement a kernel that batches copy operations for different blocks into a single kernel launch to mitigate overhead caused by discontinuous blocks in the copy-on-write mechanism.

claim_tree.statement: Inefficient memory management leads to waste via fragmentation and redundant duplication, which limits the batch size.
vLLM organizes the KV cache as fixed-size logical and physical KV blocks, analogous to pages in virtual memory.

claim.statement: vLLM organizes the KV cache as fixed-size logical and physical KV blocks, analogous to pages in virtual memory.

universal_facets.improves_or_replaces: existing systems that reserve memory in advance
universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Dynamic allocation vs. pre-reserving memory to eliminate waste

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU DRAM memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Fixed-size logical and physical KV blocks.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical blocks via Block Table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation without pre-reserving all positions in advance.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with DRAM and CPU RAM for swapping.

children.statement: A block engine allocates a contiguous chunk of GPU DRAM and divides it into physical KV blocks.
The KV block manager maintains block tables that map logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks for each request.

claim.statement: The KV block manager maintains block tables that map logical KV blocks to physical KV blocks for each request.

systems_facets.s1_resource: KV cache memory managed on GPU DRAM and CPU RAM.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Fixed-size KV blocks used for logical and physical allocation.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: LLM service memory management layer within the inference runtime.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block tables.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation without pre-reserving positions enabled by PagedAttention.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with DRAM and CPU RAM for swapping.

children.statement: Each block table entry records the corresponding physical blocks of a logical block and the number of filled positions.

claim.statement: Each block table entry records the corresponding physical blocks of a logical block and the number of filled positions.

universal_facets.core_tradeoff: Logical contiguity vs physical non-contiguity for dynamic allocation

systems_facets.s1_resource: GPU DRAM memory for KV cache storage.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation without pre-reserving all positions in advance.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with DRAM and CPU RAM for swapping.

children.statement: Separating logical and physical KV blocks allows vLLM to dynamically grow the KV cache memory without reserving it for all positions in advance.

claim.statement: Separating logical and physical KV blocks allows vLLM to dynamically grow the KV cache memory without reserving it for all positions in advance.

systems_facets.s1_resource: KV cache memory managed on GPU DRAM and CPU RAM.
systems_facets.s2_alloc_unit: Fixed-size logical and physical KV blocks.
systems_facets.s3_stack_layer: LLM inference service memory management layer.
systems_facets.s4_mapping: Logical KV blocks mapped to physical KV blocks via block table.
systems_facets.s5_policy: Dynamic allocation of physical blocks as needed without pre-reservation.
systems_facets.s6_hw_assumption: GPU workers with DRAM and CPU RAM for swapping.

claim_tree.statement: vLLM uses a recomputation technique that recomputes the KV cache when preempted sequences are rescheduled, which can be lower latency than original latency.
Using the cudaMemcpyAsync API for individual block copy operations was avoided because it leads to numerous invocations of small data movements.
claim_tree.rejected_what: Using cudaMemcpyAsync API for individual block copy operations
claim_tree.rejected_why: Leads to numerous invocations of small data movements and overhead

claim_tree.statement: On the ShareGPT dataset, vLLM sustains 1.7x–2.7x higher request rates compared to Orca (Oracle) and 2.7x–8x compared to Orca (Max) while maintaining similar latencies.
Compared to FasterTransformer, vLLM can sustain up to 22x higher request rates on the ShareGPT dataset.
For OPT-13B on the ShareGPT dataset, vLLM processes 2.2x more requests at the same time than Orca (Oracle) and 4.3x more requests than Orca (Max).
On the Alpaca dataset, vLLM follows a similar trend to ShareGPT, though the advantage over Orca (Oracle) and Orca (Pow2) is less pronounced in Fig. 12 (f) due to compute-bound conditions.
As the request rate increases, latency initially increases at a gradual pace but then suddenly explodes when the request rate surpasses the capacity of the serving system.
However, these general systems fail to take into account the autoregressive property and token state of LLM inference, resulting in missed opportunities for optimization.
claim_tree.rejected_what: autoregressive property and token state of LLM inference
claim_tree.rejected_why: general systems fail to take into account these properties

claim_tree.statement: While both systems aim to increase the GPU utilization and hence the throughput of LLM serving, Orca achieves it by scheduling and interleaving the requests so that more requests can be processed in parallel, while vLLM is doing so by increasing memory utilization so that the working sets of more requests fit into memory.
claim_tree.rejected_why: vLLM uses increasing memory utilization instead

claim_tree.statement: By reducing memory fragmentation and enabling sharing, vLLM runs more requests in a batch in parallel and achieves a 2-4 × speedup compared to Orca.
claim_tree.rejected_why: vLLM achieves 2-4 × speedup due to memory fragmentation reduction and sharing

claim_tree.statement: Indeed, the fine-grained scheduling and interleaving of the requests like in Orca makes memory management more challenging, making the techniques proposed in vLLM even more crucial.
claim_tree.rejected_what: fine-grained scheduling and interleaving of the requests

claim_tree.statement: Notably, FlexGen [ 46 ] studies how to swap weights and token states for LLM inference with limited GPU memory, but it does not target the online serving settings.
claim_tree.rejected_why: FlexGen does not target the online serving settings

claim_tree.statement: OLLA [ 48 ] optimizes the lifetime and location of tensors to reduce fragmentation, but it does not do finegrained block-level management or online serving.
claim_tree.rejected_what: finegrained block-level management or online serving
claim_tree.rejected_why: OLLA does not do finegrained block-level management or online serving

claim_tree.statement: FlashAttention [ 13 ] applies tiling and kernel optimizations to reduce the peak memory of attention computation and reduce I/O costs. This paper introduces a new idea of block-level memory management in the context of online serving.
claim_tree.rejected_why: This paper introduces a new idea of block-level memory management instead

negative_claims.statement: Using the cudaMemcpyAsync API for individual block copy operations was avoided because it leads to numerous invocations of small data movements.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Using cudaMemcpyAsync API for individual block copy operations
negative_claims.rejected_why: Leads to numerous invocations of small data movements and overhead

negative_claims.statement: PagedAttention GPU kernels incur 20–26% higher attention kernel latency compared to FasterTransformer.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Lower attention kernel latency (relative to FasterTransformer)
negative_claims.rejected_why: Extra overheads of accessing the block table, executing extra branches, and handling variable sequence lengths

negative_claims.statement: However, this does not generally hold for every GPU workload. In such scenarios, introducing the vLLM’s techniques may rather degrade the performance due to the extra overhead of memory indirection and non-contiguous block memory.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Generalization to all GPU workloads (e.g., DNN training, non-LLM serving)
negative_claims.rejected_why: Static tensor shapes, compute-bound performance, overhead of memory indirection

negative_claims.statement: For example, in DNN training, the tensor shapes are typically static, and thus memory allocation can be optimized ahead of time.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Applicability of vLLM techniques to DNN training

negative_claims.statement: For another example, in serving DNNs that are not LLMs, an increase in memory efficiency may not result in any performance improvement since the performance is primarily compute-bound.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Performance improvement from memory efficiency in non-LLM serving

negative_claims.statement: In such scenarios, introducing the vLLM’s techniques may rather degrade the performance due to the extra overhead of memory indirection and non-contiguous block memory.
negative_claims.rejected_why: Extra overhead of memory indirection and non-contiguous block memory

negative_claims.statement: However, we would be excited to see vLLM’s techniques being applied to other workloads with similar properties to LLM serving.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Applicability to workloads without similar properties
negative_claims.rejected_why: Lack of similar properties to LLM serving

negative_claims.statement: Another example is the recomputation method to recover the evicted blocks, which is not feasible in OS.
Besides, vLLM mitigates the overhead of memory indirection in paging by fusing the GPU kernels for memory access operations with those for other operations such as attention.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Overhead of memory indirection in paging
negative_claims.rejected_why: Fusing GPU kernels for memory access operations

negative_claims.statement: One example is vLLM’s all-or-nothing swap-out policy, which exploits the fact that processing a request requires all of its corresponding token states to be stored in GPU memory.
negative_claims.rejected_why: Processing a request requires all of its corresponding token states to be stored in GPU memory

negative_claims.statement: vLLM re-interprets and augments the idea of virtual memory and paging by leveraging the applicationspecific semantics.
negative_claims.rejected_what: Standard virtual memory and paging idea
negative_claims.rejected_why: vLLM re-interprets and augments it by leveraging applicationspecific semantics

negative_claims.statement: vLLM achieves 2-4 × throughput improvements over the state-of-the-art systems.
vLLM is a high-throughput LLM serving system with efficient memory management enabled by PagedAttention.
